TextENGNew: ``Oh, here she comes!@k@vS09/01/170100178. Mornin', Rena!``
changeReason: Old fabricates 'You're late today!' not in the Japanese.

TextENGNew: ``Ahahahaha, you beat me today, Keiichi-kun.``
``I'm not saying I'm usually any better, but me beating Rena to the meeting spot? That's gotta be a first.``
``Hau,@k@vS09/02/170200227. I overslept a little this morning.``
``I couldn't fall asleep last night.``

``You too?@k@vS09/01/170100181. Nahahaha, same here actually.@k@vS09/01/170100182. The high from yesterday just wouldn't fade.@k@vS09/01/170100183. I was lying in bed forever but couldn't get to sleep—it felt like the night went on forever.``
Yesterday was such a blast with everyone, and I'd gone home still riding the excitement.
My stomach was so full I could barely touch dinner.
I'd spent the whole evening telling my parents just how much fun I'd had.
Too excited to sleep because I had too much fun—what am I, a kindergartner?
But Rena had been the same way, so I felt better about it.
...No, "felt better" isn't quite right. I should say I was glad to know we'd shared the same feeling.

Rena must have gone treasure hunting at the junk heaps after I left, found all sorts of kyute things, and headed home in high spirits,@k chattering endlessly to her family about what a wonderful day it had been.
changeReason: Changed 'adorable' to 'kyute'; adopts the series-specific rendering of Rena's catchphrase かぁいい

TextENGNew: ``Oh, it's Mii-chan!@k@vS09/02/170200230. Good morning!!``
``Whoa, Mion beat us here? It might actually snow today!``
changeReason: Old substitutes 'pigs falling from the sky' for JPN's 'snow today' idiom. Both express disbelief that Mion arrived early, but new preserves the original snow imagery.

TextENGNew: ``I get here early for once and that's what you say?!``
changeReason: Changed perspective from 'I usually make you wait' to 'I get here early for once'; new version matches JPN 珍しく先に待ってりゃ (unusually waiting first)

TextENGNew: ``Ahaha, speaking of which, Keiichi-kun was here before me too.``
``Maybe it really will snow☆``
changeReason: Old fabricates 'Today is full of miracles!'; Japanese says 'Maybe it really will snow.'

TextENGNew: ``Ahahaha!@k@vS09/03/170300064. Look who's talking!``
Sure enough, another fun, ordinary day was beginning.
The day before yesterday was fun.@k Yesterday was fun.@k So today would definitely be fun too.
I was happy and having a great time, and Mion was happy and having a great time.
And of course, Rena and everyone else were happy too.
I found myself honestly wishing these happy days would go on forever.@k That's what I genuinely felt this morning.
``Hey, Mion.@k@vS09/01/170100186. What should we play today?``
``For club today?``
``Hmmmm, what should we do?@k@vS09/03/170300067. Rena, got any suggestions?``
``Let's see...``
``We've had physical games two days in a row, so maybe something less strenuous today.``
``Good point.@k@vS09/01/170100188. That's a solid idea, Rena.@k@vS09/01/170100189. Club activities aren't all about physical endurance, right?``
``Of course not!@k@vS09/03/170300069. A true club member must excel in mind, technique, and body—all three!``
changeReason: Changed 'brains, skill, and brawn' to 'mind, technique, and body'; new version correctly translates 心技体 (shin-gi-tai), the traditional Japanese concept

TextENGNew: ``Then let's play some tabletop games today.``
Since everyone had arrived early this morning,@k we actually got to enjoy a leisurely walk to school for once.
Before long, the conversation turned to the upcoming festival.
It was called the Watanagashi festival, and apparently it was the liveliest annual event in Hinamizawa.
``Oh right, yesterday my relatives gathered at our place in Okinomiya and we were talking about all sorts of things.@k@vS09/03/170300072. Your name came up, Kei-chan.``
``M-Me?@k@vS09/01/170100191. What would the big shots of the Sonozaki family be discussing about me...?``
``Well, you see...@k@vS09/03/170300074. My Uncle Yoshirou—he's the manager of Angel Mort—saw your performance yesterday,@k@vS09/03/170300075. and he's taken quite a liking to you.``
``So I started asking around, and it turns out a surprising number of the relatives already knew about you!@k@vS09/03/170300077. They were calling you the famous man of Hinamizawa.``
changeReason: Added 'man' to match JPN 名物男 (notable/famous man); changes from vague fame to the specific Japanese expression

TextENGNew: ``F-Famous man, huh? That's a pretty dubious title...@k@vS09/01/170100193. I bet ninety percent of that fame comes from your punishment games.``
changeReason: Added 'ninety percent' matching JPN ９割方 (ninety percent); old version lost this specific quantifier

TextENGNew: ``That's not all.@k@vS09/03/170300079. They said you've got great spirit, tons of energy, and a reckless streak—they were genuinely impressed, saying it's rare to find a young person like that these days.``
Being praised by people I'd never even met felt... kind of embarrassing, actually.
``So then, they were wondering if you'd take on some kind of role at the upcoming Watanagashi festival.@k@vS09/03/170300081. You like being in the spotlight, don't you, Kei-chan?``
``Hey, hey, hey!@k@vS09/01/170100195. Don't go making plans without me there!@k@vS09/01/170100196. I'm a quiet, solitary guy who loves his peace and loneliness, you know?``
``Ahahahahahaha!!@k@vS09/03/170300083. Who's a lonely guy?!``
``You mean "loan-ly guy," don't you?@k@vS09/03/170300085. Heh heh heh!``
``Hau~, loans, Keiichi-kun...@k@vS09/02/170200236. If you're in financial trouble, we can help... hau...``
``Whooooaaah!!@k@vS09/01/170100198. I'm not that broke! And Rena, don't take that seriously and look at me with those pitying eyes!!``
We were clearly still riding the high from yesterday.
We hadn't even reached school yet and Mion and I were already going at it.
Rena watched us and laughed happily.
Once we arrived at school, Satoko and Rika-chan were exactly the same.
Tomita and Okamura—the underclassman duo who'd joined in the chaos with us—were no different.
Everyone had been riding this wave of fun without pause.@k Here, everyone was happy.
``...It hasn't even been a year since I moved here, but...``
``Hm? What's up all of a sudden?``
``No, it's just... since moving to Hinamizawa, I've really become happy, you know?``
``Ahahaha!@k@vS09/03/170300089. What's gotten into you, Kei-chan? Going all sentimental on us~``

``I understand, Keiichi-kun.@k@vS09/02/170200238. The feeling that everything became happy after moving to Hinamizawa—I understand it completely.@k@vS09/02/170200239. ...I think this village must have some kind of magic that makes people happy.``
``Are you happy, Rena?``
``Yes.@k@vS09/02/170200241. Every day since moving to Hinamizawa has been a happy one.``
``Yeah, same here.``
``...H-Hey!@k@vS09/03/170300091. Don't leave this old man out of it!@k@vS09/03/170300092. That's not fair!``
The sky was clear, and the wisps of drifting clouds were dazzlingly white.
The cicadas' chorus signaled that summer was arriving much earlier than usual.
Watanagashi was coming up soon.
It was sure to be a wonderful festival.
And together with my friends, I'd go on to have even more fun times ahead.
The true arrival of summer was right around the corner.
Classes finally ended, and it was time for after-school club activities.
Today's session was sure to be fun.
I'd make the most of it with everyone.
But then Mii-chan got a phone call—something had come up with her part-time job, and club was cancelled.
...Tsk.@k ...I was really looking forward to it.
Well, nothing to be done about it.@k At least I could have a nice chat with Keiichi-kun on the walk home.
He can be a bit mean sometimes, but Keiichi-kun is really entertaining.
When I'm talking with him, before I know it, the gloom lifts right off my mind.
...Wait.
Am I... gloomy...?
Every day is fun and I'm as happy as can be.@k ...What do I have to feel gloomy about?
...I know what it is.
Thanks to Rina-san, Dad has perked up so much.
Before he met Rina-san, at best he was a dim lamp in broad daylight.
At worst, he was nothing but a listless corpse.
But since he started seeing Rina-san, he'd begun wearing a smile like the one he used to have when he still lived happily with Mom.
Chatting with Rina-san, going out with her.@k ...Dates, I suppose.@k Dad looked ten or twenty years younger.
Rina-san gets along wonderfully with Dad, and... she's kind to me too.
There's really... no reason for me to dislike her.
But... the bitter memories of my parents' divorce, and all the regrets I'd gone over again and again, kept gnawing away at my heart.
Was it really okay for me to accept that woman?
Could Rina-san possibly be...@k ...the same kind of person Uncle Akihito turned out to be?
Even if she personally bore no ill will—
could she be an "enemy," someone whose very existence would steal my happiness?
Was I going to drift along obliviously until another catastrophic collapse?
Shouldn't I fight the "enemy"... before it's too late?
Because Rina-san was... coming and going from our house far too freely.
Ever since Dad started letting Rina-san stay over, her personal belongings had been multiplying in the house little by little.
Neither Dad nor I smoke, yet somehow an ashtray had appeared in the living room.
The futon in the guest room had quietly become Rina-san's.
In the bathroom, there was a toothbrush I'd never choose,@k and in the shower, a shampoo I'd never pick.
Around the time those things started to bother me,@k ...I began to notice it the moment I stepped through the front door coming home.@k Her perfume.
I'd once asked Dad point-blank if he planned to marry Rina-san.
He'd made a face that was half embarrassed, half troubled, and answered vaguely: not yet.
He knew I'd been wounded by Mom's divorce.
That's probably why he couldn't bring up remarriage with me.
...That's why it came out as "not yet."
Someday he might consider remarrying, but not while I was still living with him.@k That was what he meant.
There was only one reason I didn't want him to remarry Rina-san.
The thought of a stranger violating the sacred space of my family repulsed me.
But maybe that was just my selfishness.
Because keeping Dad all to myself meant@k ...keeping him trapped in that dark, broken state he'd been in before Rina-san came along.
Dad has his own life to live.
It's his right to heal from the divorce and pursue a new love.
In the first place, if I'd managed to prevent Mom's divorce, Dad would never have had to endure such a painful life.
So Dad deserved a happy new life—one that would let him forget pain he should never have had to bear—and I had absolutely no right to stand in its way.
...If anything, I had an obligation to work toward restoring his happiness.
...I had to atone for the sin of failing to prevent the divorce.
Then what should I do so that both Dad and I could be happy?
...It wasn't that difficult.
All I had to do was silently accept Dad and Rina-san's relationship.
Dad said he wouldn't remarry while I was still living with him.
So I just needed to break free of my dependence on "family" and become independent as soon as possible.
But that didn't mean I could ask him to hold off seeing Rina-san until that day.
Rina-san squeezed in visits between her work shifts, and when their schedules aligned, she even stayed the night.@k ...They were adults.@k I had no standing to say anything.
So.
I chose to silently accept Dad and Rina-san's relationship@k ...and to avoid crossing paths with Rina-san whenever possible.
On the surface, Rina-san and I got along fine.
...If we didn't, Dad would be torn trying to keep both of us happy.
But maintaining a friendly facade with someone I couldn't bring myself to like was exhausting and painful.
So whenever I knew Rina-san was coming over, I'd spend as much time as possible outside and come home late.
Mii-chan's club activities were the perfect way to kill time.@k But they didn't happen every day.
So on days without club, I'd go to the trash heaps and treasure-hunt alone to pass the hours.
And that's how... I'd built my hideout in the junk piles.
Dad and Rina-san could enjoy each other's company at home.
And I could avoid forcing myself to face them, spending my time instead in my hideout, being gentle with my own heart.
...Sometimes, this life did make me feel depressed.
Sometimes I wanted to pour my heart out to someone.
But... what good would talking do?@k This was the best I could manage.
The most important thing for me right now was to get past the grief of losing my family as my own and to seize my own happiness.
After all, every day was so much fun, so full of happiness.
...Even now, just on the walk home, Keiichi-kun had been telling me one funny story after another, never letting me be bored.
Filling my head with gloomy thoughts while he was doing that felt rude to him.
Dad would find happiness in his own way.
And I would find happiness in mine.
The pain I felt was simply because I hadn't weaned myself from my father yet.
Compared to the shock of Mom's divorce, Rina-san's encroachment was the most gradual change imaginable.
And I think Rina-san understood my feelings too.
She was kind to me, but didn't push beyond what was necessary.
She probably understood that being overly familiar would only make me uncomfortable.
I admired Rika-chan, living so bravely after losing both parents.
Compared to the agony of having both parents ripped away so suddenly, my problems were utterly trivial.
I admired Satoko-chan's resilience too—losing her parents, then being forced into a harsh life under her uncle and aunt.
Compared to what Satoko-chan endured under her uncle's cruelty, how blessed my relationship with Rina-san was.
...I only felt anxious because I was still immature, unsettled by the pace at which I was being pushed toward independence.
I should be as strong as Rika-chan and Satoko-chan.
I should do my best and fight to seize my own happiness.
I would live with all my might... to reclaim the peace of mind I'd lost that day.
I was only unhappy because I'd convinced myself I was.
I should savor these wonderful days spent with wonderful friends.
Just by realizing I was happy, the world shone so brilliantly.
``And then, and then!@k@vS09/02/170200244. It was just sooo kyute that I went hau~, I'm taking it hooome!! ☆``
``Ahahahaha... you're hopeless.@k@vS09/01/170100202. Rena, you're a true master of making every day count.``
``Every day is fun for me, you know?@k@vS09/02/170200246. Surrounded by such wonderful friends, living such wonderful days—how could I possibly feel unhappy?``
``Exactly.@k@vS09/01/170100204. There's no unhappiness here.@k@vS09/01/170100205. It's fun all the time, with no room for misfortune!``
``That's what Hinamizawa is!``
He should have stopped right there.@k But Keiichi-kun added one careless remark.
``I'm jealous of you, Rena.@k@vS09/01/170100208. I envy that carefree, worry-free attitude of yours!``

``...Hau...@k@vS09/02/170200248. That sounds like you're making fun of me......@k@vS09/02/170200249. But, if being carefree is what it takes to make every day wonderful... I wouldn't mind.@k@vS09/02/170200250. ...Would I?``
I don't have any worries.
What feels like worry is just my own immaturity—my inability to let go of my father.
Come on.
Say the magic words.@k They'll cheer you right up.
It had nothing to do with what we'd been talking about. But these were my magic words—the spell I used to lift my own spirits.
``Hauu~~☆@k@vS09/02/170200253. I'm taking it hooome!!!``
``Ahahahaha, what was that?@k@vS09/01/170100211. But hey, that's so you, Rena!``
That's right.@k This is who I am. Rena.
Not Reina, the girl who still clings to her parents.
...Rena is a bit silly, carefree, excitable—but she knows that her days are filled with happiness, and she has the kindness to be grateful for it.
I should forget all the "icky" things.
Forget them all and start walking a wonderful life, one free of anything "icky."
That's why I dropped the "i."@k ...I threw it away.
I'm going to reclaim, right here in Hinamizawa, the happiness I lost when I left.
I will overcome pain and hardship.
I can be happy.
Because I've already thrown away all the "i"-cky things.
From here on, only good things await.
...Oh.@k ..."Incredible" starts with "i" too.
My house was coming into view... and that's when I noticed something different.
A truck was parked in front of the house.
The bed was empty, but scattered cardboard, blankets, and tie-down ropes made it obvious something had been delivered.
From the direction of the front door, I heard two young men's voices in unison: "Well then, we'll be going!"
I passed them on my way to the entrance.
They were wearing work uniforms—movers, by the look of them.
Dad had just seen the visitors off and was about to close the door when our eyes met.
``I'm home.@k@vS09/02/170200253_2. ...What's going on?``
``Welcome back, Reina.@k@vS09/00/172900002. Come look—it's amazing!``
Dad was in extremely high spirits.
I didn't know why, but whenever he beamed like that, it made me want to smile back.
This was the same father whose eyes had looked like a dead fish's, no matter what smile I gave him.
And now he could light up with a grin just to surprise me.
In the year since we'd moved to Hinamizawa—no, since he'd started seeing Rina-san—Dad had quite literally come back to life.
When I saw him like this, I felt happy, and at the same time, confused about whether I should be grateful to Rina-san.
``Look, isn't it wonderful?``
Dad ushered me eagerly into the living room.
He was clearly expecting me to gasp in delight.
So whatever I found in the living room, I was ready to gasp in delight for him.
``...Wow... Wh-What is this?@k@vS09/02/170200255. ...D-Dad, what did you do?!``
I let out a voice pitched between surprise and wonder.
Standing behind me, Dad was surely smiling with satisfaction at the sound.
But behind that voice, I was... desperately trying to make sense of what I was looking at.
For a moment, I doubted this was even our house.
Because the living room had been completely redecorated.
Most of our furniture had come from our old house in Ibaraki.
My parents had bought it when they got married, so most of it was old and worn.
Some pieces even still had crayon doodles I'd scrawled as a little girl.
Every single one of those old things was gone.
The whole room was done in pastel tones, giving it the air of a stylish urban apartment from a TV drama.
The carpet had been replaced with something tropical.
The curtains seemed to match.
The sofa was something sleek and fashionable.
...The whole room looked like a resort hotel.
Our battered old TV—the one with the broken channel knob that we'd had to change with pliers—had been replaced by a large, modern flat-screen.
Even the light fixtures had been swapped out for something stylish.
The pristine white light of brand-new fluorescent bulbs illuminated the room as if it were a different world.
``Th-This is... amazing, Dad.@k@vS09/02/170200257. What brought this on?``
``A fresh start.@k@vS09/00/172900005. It's been a whole year since we moved here, so I figured it was time to shake things up and really commit to this new life.``
I'd actually suggested redecorating once before, to cheer him up while he was still reeling from the divorce.
But in his depressed state, he'd shown no interest.
...Even after everything, he still couldn't bring himself to part with the old furniture he and Mom must have chosen together—some part of him wasn't ready to move on.
So I'd been planning to suggest it again when the timing felt right...@k I never imagined he'd do it on his own.
``That's great.@k@vS09/02/170200259. Ahahahaha, a beautiful room like this really lifts your spirits, doesn't it?@k@vS09/02/170200260. I think it's wonderful!``
``Right?@k@vS09/00/172900007. Hey Reina, you always wanted a rocking chair, didn't you?@k@vS09/00/172900008. I haven't assembled it yet, but look!@k@vS09/00/172900009. I got one!``
``Hauuu!!@k@vS09/02/170200262. Dad, that's amazing! Amazing! Amazing!@k@vS09/02/170200263. Can I really have it? Really?!``
``Easy now, it's for everyone to share. It's not just yours, Reina.@k@vS09/00/172900011. Ahahahaha!``
``Shared or not, I'm going to monopolize it~@k@vS09/02/170200265. Ahahahaha.``
Dad proudly walked me through every detail of the redecorated living room.
Apparently it was just the living room for now, but he planned to redo the other rooms eventually too.
I couldn't believe how energetic and proactive he'd become.
The taste was a little questionable, but if it made Dad happy, that was a wonderful thing.
Glancing out at the yard, I noticed... the old furniture had been set out there.
``Are you... going to throw those away?``
``Yeah, I figured now was the time.@k@vS09/00/172900013. A hauler is coming later to pick them up.``
Getting rid of the old furniture was also a farewell to memories of my mother—the bad person.
...Hadn't there been a time when I myself had despised those things for smelling like Mom and tried to smash them?
I could understand Dad wanting to dispose of them and move on.
But... for some reason, I felt like there was no place for me in this new living room.
This room's aesthetic... wasn't Dad's taste.@k It was probably Rina-san's.
No doubt Dad and Rina-san had planned the remodel together and picked out the furniture at some department store.
...I tried to take it charitably—he hadn't told me because he wanted it to be a surprise.
...No... Dad wasn't the kind of person who thought that deeply about things.
He hadn't told me simply because it hadn't occurred to him that he needed to.
...The idea of remaking this house in the image of Dad and Rina-san while erasing my place in it was absolutely not his intention.
I shouldn't think such negative things...
Dad didn't have any ill intentions... he wasn't trying to push me out... he didn't see me as an obstacle to remarriage...
And yet.
...Why did I feel so sentimental, seeing the furniture of the mother I despised sitting out in the yard?@k Why did this living room feel like it wasn't my place?
The answer... came to me immediately.
It was me.
For Dad, wanting to throw away everything that reminded him of Mom and start fresh might be... an unconscious expression of wanting to discard her daughter as well.
...Dad doesn't think deeply about things, so he probably isn't consciously thinking of me as an obstacle.
...But he understands that he can't remarry as long as I'm living with him.
...In the end, just by being here, I'm causing Dad pain.
The divorce was my fault...
So I have to support Dad in reclaiming his happiness.
I can't keep clinging to him just because I want him all to myself.
I need to seriously think about becoming independent.
...Maybe I should forget about going to high school and think about getting a job instead.
But without a proper education, jobs don't come easy.
Let alone earning enough to live on my own.
...And whether it's even a job I want to do... I probably wouldn't have the luxury of choosing.
Only people who've put in the effort get to choose their profession.
...If it's about earning enough to survive... maybe I can't afford to be picky about appearances...@k ...But...
Where... do I belong?
At times like this, my hideout in the trash heaps feels unbearably dear to me.
Wrapped in a blanket, feeling warmth against the too-cool night—there's a tenderness in that beyond words.
Hot and muggy by day, cold by night—an uncomfortable hideout.
...And yet, it might now be the place where I could relax more than... my own home.
No.@k I shouldn't think like this.
Don't think of Rina-san as invading my home.@k Be grateful that she's made Dad happy.@k Rina-san isn't trying to push me out.
She's actually trying her best to get along with me.@k It's me who keeps refusing to open up, who keeps running away.
I'm the one at fault. I'm the one at fault.
And I'm the one who's blessed.@k I'm blessed.
After all... compared to Satoshi-kun's relationship with his aunt—that relentless, unending abuse—my situation is so much easier.

When I think of what Satoko-chan and Rika-chan have been through, losing their families and clinging together to survive, how blessed I am to be able to come home at my own pace and spend time with my father.
Thinking of myself as unhappy over something this minor would be an insult.
I am happy.@k I am still blessed.
The trouble with me is that I can't bring myself to accept that happiness...
Dad was still going on proudly about the trials and tribulations of redecorating.
I nodded along with my brightest smile, encouraging him to keep talking.
``Oh right, I almost forgot.@k@vS09/00/172900015. Reina, could I send you on an errand?``
``An errand?@k@vS09/02/170200268. Sure, what is it?``

``I ordered a couple of jackets from a clothing store.@k@vS09/00/172900017. They came in today, apparently.@k@vS09/00/172900018. I need to help the haulers with the furniture, so could you go pick them up for me?``
``Sure, no problem.@k@vS09/02/170200270. Which store?@k@vS09/02/170200271. I wonder what kind of jackets you got.@k@vS09/02/170200272. I can't wait to see!``
Whatever the jackets looked like, Rina-san had probably helped pick them out.
But I didn't mind running the errand at all.
Frankly, going out seemed far more bearable than sitting in this living room listening to more of Dad's boasting.
I took the slip, confirmed the shop's location, changed my clothes, and headed out.
...Stepping outside, I pushed my bicycle through the yard and took one more look at the old furniture.
Those things had once been objects of my hatred, and yet now, for some reason... they stirred a pang of sentiment in me.
...Was this feeling an attachment to my mother—one I couldn't sever despite my hatred?
If I surrendered my heart to these emotions any longer, they would surely become poison.
...I knew that rationally. But I couldn't shake the feeling.
The yard didn't only hold discarded furniture.
There was also plenty of junk I'd brought home from my treasure hunts on a whim.
Those would probably be thrown away too.
Dad had always hated the junk I collected.@k He'd surely have the haulers take it all along with the old furniture.
...Just as an animal marks its territory with its scent to feel secure and at home,@k perhaps piling up this junk had been my way of confirming that I was allowed to exist here.
No, that's not it.
...These things, abandoned by those who made them—
maybe I wanted to bring them to a place where they were allowed to belong.
